concept_name: Acceleration-Time Graphs — Area Gives Change in Velocity
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.5 — a-t graphs; split from motion_graphs bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A Chennai city bus pulling away from a red light. The speedometer climbs smoothly from 0 to 40 km/h in about eight seconds — roughly steady throttle, so acceleration is nearly constant at 1.4 m/s^2. On an a-t graph that is a flat horizontal line at 1.4. The area under that flat line over 8 seconds is 11.2 m/s — exactly the change in velocity the passengers felt as the bus picked up.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A Hero Splendor bike rider on an empty NH-44 stretch. She opens the throttle in short bursts, then eases off. The acceleration itself rises and falls with each throttle kick — the a-t graph looks like a row of hills and valleys. Each hill's area equals the velocity gained during that burst. Each valley below the time axis means she applied the brakes.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: An ISRO rocket at liftoff. In the first few seconds acceleration rises sharply as thrust overcomes weight. Mid-flight it climbs further because the rocket is shedding fuel mass. The a-t curve is a rising function, not a constant. Integrating that curve over the booster burn gives the huge velocity change that reaches orbital speed.

delta_v.name: Change in velocity = area under a-t graph

formulas.area_is_delta_v: Area between a-t curve and t-axis over [t1, t2] = change in velocity v(t2) - v(t1)
formulas.flat_line_a_t: Horizontal a-t line -> constant acceleration -> linear v-t graph
formulas.slope_is_jerk: Slope of a-t graph = da/dt = rate of change of acceleration (jerk)
formulas.zero_a_t: If a-t is zero everywhere, velocity is constant and displacement grows linearly

constraints: Horizontal a-t line at zero -> zero acceleration -> constant velocity
Horizontal a-t line at non-zero -> uniform acceleration
Rising a-t line -> accelerating harder each second
a-t line below the time axis -> deceleration (braking)

STATE_1.title: Reading an a-t Graph — The Flat Horizontal Line

scene_composition.text:
Acceleration-Time graph (a vs t)

a |
  |
  |___________________________
  |        a = 1.4 m/s^2
  |
  |
  |______________________________ t
  0        2s      4s      6s     8s

  Height of the line = acceleration at that moment.
  Flat horizontal line = constant acceleration.

  Chennai city bus:
    t=0       red light turns green
    t=8 s     bus has reached 40 km/h
    acceleration  =  ~1.4 m/s^2  (steady)
Chennai city bus pulling away from a red light

tts_sentences.text_en: The acceleration-time graph plots the instantaneous acceleration against the clock.
Its vertical axis is no longer metres or metres per second — it is metres per second squared.
If the line is a flat horizontal strip at 1.4, it means the object is accelerating at exactly 1.4 metres per second squared, every second.
Picture a Chennai city bus leaving a red light. The driver keeps her throttle steady, so the bus accelerates uniformly — the a-t line is a flat horizontal line.
Flat here does NOT mean standstill. It means steady acceleration. The velocity is still climbing; it is the RATE of climb that is constant.

STATE_2.title: Area Under an a-t Graph = Change in Velocity

scene_composition.text:
Area under a-t curve = change in velocity

a |
  |                ________
  |               |        |
  |               |  area  |
  |               |  =     |
  |               | delta_v|
  |_______________|________|__________  t
  0              t1       t2

  Units check:
     strip height = m/s^2
     strip width  = s
     strip area   = m/s^2 * s  = m/s

  So area under an a-t graph is a VELOCITY change,
  not a displacement.

  Bus example (continued):
     constant a = 1.4 m/s^2, time = 8 s
     delta_v  =  1.4 * 8  =  11.2 m/s  ~  40 km/h
Area = delta_v (change in velocity over the interval)
Area Under an a-t Graph = Change in Velocity

tts_sentences.text_en: Just as the area under a v-t graph gave distance, the area under an a-t graph gives change in velocity.
Dimensionally it is unavoidable: height is metres per second squared, width is seconds, so the area is metres per second — a velocity.
Back to our Chennai bus. Acceleration is 1.4 metres per second squared, sustained for 8 seconds.
The area is simply 1.4 times 8, which is 11 point 2 metres per second — exactly the speed that bus picked up, about 40 kilometres per hour.
So an a-t graph answers a different question than v-t: it tells you how much faster the object got, not how far it travelled.

STATE_3.title: When a-t Dips Below the Axis — Braking

scene_composition.text:
a-t graph dipping below t-axis

a |
  |   throttle up         coast
  |   _________           ________
  |  |         |         |        |
  |  |  +a     |         |  ~ 0  |
  |__|_________|_________|________|______  t
  |             |
  |             |
  |             |_________________
  |                      BRAKING
  |                      area here is NEGATIVE
  |                      -> velocity decreases
  |
  Negative area means velocity is FALLING.
  If braking area equals earlier positive area,
  the bike can come to a complete stop.
NH-44 bike ride: throttle burst, coast, then brake

tts_sentences.text_en: The a-t graph carries a sign, and that sign carries real physical meaning.
When the curve sits above the time axis the object is speeding up. Positive area means positive gain in velocity.
When the curve drops below the time axis the object is braking. The area there counts as negative, meaning velocity is being subtracted.
A bike on NH-44: she burns throttle in a burst, coasts at zero acceleration, then brakes — first a positive rectangle, then nothing, then a negative rectangle.
If the negative braking area equals the earlier positive area, the bike ends exactly at the speed she started with. Sign accounting is the secret of the a-t graph.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student thinks a flat horizontal line on an a-t graph means the object is at rest.
confusion_patterns: flat line on at graph means at rest
horizontal at graph means no motion
is object at rest if a is constant

STATE_1.title: The Trap: Flat a-t Looks Like 'Nothing is Happening'

scene_composition.text:
Student's wrong reading:

a |
  |
  |_____________________
  |    flat at a = 2
  |
  |_________________________ t

  "The line is flat, so nothing is changing,
   so the object must be at rest."

  That confuses 'constant acceleration'
  with 'zero velocity'. They are completely different.
The Trap: Flat a-t Looks Like 'Nothing is Happening'
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the single most common mistake with a-t graphs.
A student sees a flat horizontal line and says: the graph is not changing, so nothing is moving.
A flat a-t line is about the ACCELERATION not changing. It does not say anything about whether the object is moving, fast or slow.
In fact a flat a-t line almost always comes paired with a velocity that is steadily increasing.

STATE_2.title: Correct Reading: Flat a-t Means Steady Acceleration

scene_composition.text:
Correct reading:

a |                                v |         ____
  |___________________             |       /
  |   a = 2 m/s^2                  |     /
  |                                |   /
  |                                | /
  |_________________________ t     |/________________ t

  Flat line on a-t  ->  slope on v-t is constant  ->  v rises linearly.
  
  So 'flat a-t' does not mean 'flat v-t'.
  It means 'straight sloping v-t'.
Correct Reading: Flat a-t Means Steady Acceleration

tts_sentences.text_en: Translate an a-t graph into a v-t graph to see what is really happening.
A flat horizontal a-t line at value 2 means every second, velocity gains 2 metres per second.
On the v-t graph, that is a straight sloping line going up — very much NOT a flat line at zero.
So the car with a flat a-t line at 2 is not parked. Its speedometer needle is climbing steadily, second after second.

STATE_3.title: When a-t Really Does Mean 'At Rest'

scene_composition.text:
When a-t + v-t together show 'at rest':

a |                                v |
  |____________________            |
  |     a = 0                      |  v = 0 everywhere
  |                                |_____________________ t
  |_________________________ t     |

  You need BOTH:
     a = 0  (flat a-t at zero level)  AND
     v = 0  (flat v-t at zero level)

  Only then is the object truly at rest.
  A flat a-t alone is not enough.
When a-t Really Does Mean 'At Rest'
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: If the question asks whether an object is at rest, an a-t graph alone is not enough to answer.
You need the a-t graph AND the v-t graph to both be flat at zero.
Zero acceleration plus zero velocity means the object is stationary. Zero acceleration plus non-zero velocity means it is cruising.
Always pair the two graphs when you are asked about the state of motion. Never read an a-t in isolation.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Belief: Flat At Means Stationary (auto-generated from branch_id — needs author rewrite).

0.label: Steady acceleration of 2 m/s^2 for 8 s

1.label: Heavy acceleration of 5 m/s^2 for 6 s
